title: Making appointments
description: Learn how to suggest, accept, decline, and confirm appointments â€” by phone, email, and in conversation.

content.title: Suggesting a time â€” language for making appointments
content.body:
When making an appointment, you suggest a time and check if the other person is free.

Suggesting:
â€¢ Are you free on Tuesday at 3 PM?
â€¢ Would Wednesday morning work for you?
â€¢ How does Thursday afternoon suit you?
â€¢ Shall we say 2 o'clock on Friday?
â€¢ Could we meet sometime next week?

Accepting:
â€¢ Yes, that works for me.
â€¢ Tuesday at 3 is perfect.
â€¢ That suits me well.
â€¢ I'll put it in my calendar.

Declining politely:
â€¢ I'm afraid I'm not free then.
â€¢ That doesn't work for me â€” could we try another time?
â€¢ I'm busy that morning â€” would afternoon be possible?
content.grammar_note:
Use WOULD/COULD for polite suggestions
'Shall we say...' = very natural for appointments
'I'm afraid...' = polite way to decline

content.title: Confirming and checking appointment details
content.body:
After agreeing a time, always confirm the details clearly.

Confirming:
â€¢ So, we're meeting on Tuesday at 3 PM â€” is that correct?
â€¢ Just to confirm â€” the appointment is on Friday at 10 AM.
â€¢ I'll send you a calendar invite to confirm.
â€¢ Can I just double-check the venue?

Checking details:
â€¢ Where exactly are we meeting?
â€¢ Is it at your office or mine?
â€¢ Should I call you or will you call me?
â€¢ What's the address?

Reminding:
â€¢ Just a reminder about our meeting tomorrow at 2 PM.
â€¢ Don't forget â€” we're meeting on Thursday!
content.grammar_note:
'Just to confirm...' = polite way to double check
'Just a reminder...' = gentle notification

vocabulary.meaning: a fixed time period for an appointment
vocabulary.example: There's a slot available at 4 PM.

vocabulary.meaning: the times when you are free

vocabulary.example: Please confirm the appointment by email.

vocabulary.meaning: to check something again to be sure
agreed or shared by both people
vocabulary.example: We need to find a mutually convenient time.

vocabulary.meaning: before the time of the appointment
vocabulary.example: Please book at least 24 hours in advance.

sentences: Are you free for a meeting on Wednesday at 11 AM?
That works perfectly for me â€” I'll put it in my calendar.
I'm afraid I'm busy that morning â€” would afternoon be possible?
Just to confirm â€” we're meeting on Friday at 2 PM at your office.
Could we reschedule? Something urgent has come up.

content.question: Priya wants to suggest a meeting time to a client. Which is the most professional way?
options: Would Tuesday at 3 PM suit you?
I need you at 3 PM Tuesday.
content.correct: Would Tuesday at 3 PM suit you?
content.explanation: 'Would...suit you?' is polite and professional â€” it puts the other person's convenience first.

content.question: The suggested time doesn't work for Arjun. How does he politely decline?
options: I'm afraid that time doesn't work for me â€” would Thursday be possible instead?
content.correct: I'm afraid that time doesn't work for me â€” would Thursday be possible instead?
content.explanation: 'I'm afraid...' softens the refusal. Suggesting an alternative shows you still want to meet.

content.question: After agreeing on a time, Meera wants to confirm. Which is correct?
options: Just to confirm â€” we're meeting at your office on Monday at 10 AM.
content.correct: Just to confirm â€” we're meeting at your office on Monday at 10 AM.
content.explanation: 'Just to confirm...' is the standard professional way to check all details before ending the conversation.

content.question: Which phrase is most appropriate to send the day before an appointment?
options: Just a gentle reminder about our meeting tomorrow at 2 PM. See you then!
You have to come tomorrow at 2.
content.correct: Just a gentle reminder about our meeting tomorrow at 2 PM. See you then!
content.explanation: 'Just a gentle reminder' is polite and friendly â€” perfect for professional reminders.

content.instruction: Fill the gaps to complete the appointment conversation.

sentences.text: A: ___ you free on Thursday afternoon? B: Yes, that ___ perfectly.
A: Shall we ___ 3 PM? B: 3 PM works â€” I'll ___ it in my diary.
A: Just ___ confirm â€” your office on Thursday at 3? B: That's correct â€” see you then.

content.instruction: Complete the appointment email with correct phrases.

sentences.text: I am writing to ___ a meeting to discuss the project timeline.
Would ___ afternoon suit you, or would you prefer morning?
Please ___ me know your availability and I will send a calendar invite.
I look forward to ___ from you.

content.instruction: Arrange into a polite appointment suggestion.
content.answer: Would Friday at 3 PM suit you?

content.instruction: Arrange into an appointment confirmation sentence.
content.answer: Just to confirm â€” we're meeting on Monday at 10 AM.

content.instruction: Match the situation to the correct appointment phrase.

pairs.right: Would Tuesday at 2 PM suit you?
That works perfectly â€” I'll add it to my calendar.
I'm afraid that time doesn't work â€” could we try Wednesday?
Just to confirm â€” your office, Monday at 10?
Just a reminder about our meeting tomorrow at 3 PM.

content.title: Booking a client meeting â€” advertising agency, Mumbai
content.setting: Sonal from an advertising agency is calling a potential client, Mr Nair, to arrange a meeting.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Good morning, Mr Nair. This is Sonal Mehta from Creative Spark Agency. I hope I'm not disturbing you.

dialogue.speaker: Mr Nair
dialogue.text: Not at all, Sonal. Good morning. What can I do for you?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: I was hoping we could arrange a meeting to present our campaign proposal. Would you have some time this week?

dialogue.speaker: Mr Nair
dialogue.text: This week is quite tight. Would next week be possible?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Of course. Would Tuesday afternoon suit you? Say, around 3 PM?

dialogue.speaker: Mr Nair
dialogue.text: Tuesday at 3 â€” let me just check. I'm afraid I have a board meeting until 4 on Tuesday. Would Wednesday work instead?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Wednesday is perfect. What time suits you best?

dialogue.speaker: Mr Nair
dialogue.text: How about 11 AM? I'm usually free in the mornings.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Wednesday at 11 AM works very well. Should we meet at your office or would you prefer our office?

dialogue.speaker: Mr Nair
dialogue.text: Let's do your office â€” I'd like to see your setup.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Wonderful! We're at Creative Spark, 4th floor, Bandra Kurla Complex. I'll send you the exact address and a calendar invite.

dialogue.speaker: Mr Nair
dialogue.text: Perfect. Just to confirm â€” Wednesday the 12th, 11 AM, your office?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: That's exactly right. I'll send a confirmation email shortly. We look forward to seeing you, Mr Nair!

comprehension_questions.question: Why can't Mr Nair meet on Tuesday at 3 PM?
options: He has a board meeting until 4 PM.
comprehension_questions.correct: He has a board meeting until 4 PM.

comprehension_questions.question: What time and day did they agree on?
Where will the meeting take place?
options: Sonal's office at Bandra Kurla Complex
comprehension_questions.correct: Sonal's office at Bandra Kurla Complex

sentences.wrong: Would Friday at 3 PM suits you?
sentences.correct: Would Friday at 3 PM suit you?
sentences.explanation: After WOULD: base verb without 's'. 'Suit', not 'suits'.

sentences.wrong: I afraid I'm not free on Tuesday.
sentences.correct: I'm afraid I'm not free on Tuesday.
sentences.explanation: 'I'm afraid' â€” needs the contraction or full form: 'I am afraid'. 'I afraid' is incorrect.

sentences.wrong: Just for confirm â€” we meet at 10 AM?
sentences.correct: Just to confirm â€” we're meeting at 10 AM?
sentences.explanation: 'Just to confirm' â€” use 'to', not 'for'. Also 'we're meeting' (present continuous for arranged plan).

content.scenario: You have a phone call with a doctor's clinic to book an appointment. The receptionist says Tuesday at 9 AM or Thursday at 4 PM are available. You prefer Thursday but want to confirm. What do you say?
options: Thursday 4 PM works for me. Could you confirm the address and what I need to bring?
content.correct: Thursday 4 PM works for me. Could you confirm the address and what I need to bring?
content.explanation: Accept politely + ask for relevant details. This is natural, professional appointment language.

content.instruction: Role-play making an appointment. You are calling a dentist's clinic to book an appointment for next week. Suggest a time, check availability, and confirm the details.
content.prompt: Speak through a full appointment-booking phone call with a dentist's receptionist. Cover: greeting, purpose, time suggestion, confirmation.
content.example_response: Good morning! I'd like to book an appointment with Dr Kavitha for next week if possible. I have a toothache that's been bothering me for a couple of days. Which days are available? Great â€” would Wednesday afternoon suit? Say around 3 or 4 PM? Oh, 3 PM is taken but 4:30 is free â€” 4:30 PM Wednesday works perfectly for me. Can I just confirm a few things? The clinic is still at the same address on Anna Salai, correct? And is there anything I need to bring â€” like my previous X-rays? Perfect. And is there a parking space nearby? Great, thank you. So just to confirm â€” Dr Kavitha, Wednesday the 14th at 4:30 PM. I'll be there. Should I call if anything changes? Wonderful. Thank you so much for your help!
key_phrases: Is there anything I need to bring?

content.instruction: You need to arrange a meeting with a colleague to work on a shared project. Both of you are very busy. Describe negotiating a time â€” offer 3 time options, respond to rejections, and finally confirm a time.
content.prompt: Role-play scheduling a meeting with a busy colleague. Show negotiation of time, polite decline, suggestion, and confirmation.
content.example_response: Hi Meera, I wanted to check if we could find a time to work on the Q4 presentation together. I know we're both quite busy but this needs to get done. Would Monday morning work for you â€” maybe 10 or 11 AM? Oh, you have a client call then â€” okay, no problem. What about Tuesday afternoon? Around 2 or 3 PM? You're travelling on Tuesday â€” right. How about Thursday? I'm mostly free on Thursday. Morning or afternoon â€” whatever suits you. Thursday at 2 PM works for you? Perfect! So Thursday the 17th at 2 PM â€” shall we meet in the large meeting room? The one by the pantry? Yes, that's free as far as I know. Let me book it now. So just to confirm â€” Thursday 17th, 2 PM, Meeting Room 2. I'll send a calendar invite with the agenda. See you then!

content.text:
Subject: Meeting Request â€” Project Alpha Review

Dear Priya,

I am ___ (1) to request a meeting to review the progress on Project Alpha.

___ (2) Wednesday the 19th at 2 PM ___ (3) you? Alternatively, I am also ___ (4) on Thursday morning between 10 AM and 12 PM.

The meeting should take ___ (5) 45 minutes. We could meet at the ___ (6) floor meeting room or online â€” whichever is more ___ (7) for you.

Could you please let me know your ___ (8) at your earliest convenience? I will then send a calendar ___ (9) to confirm.

Kind ___ (10),
Arjun

content.title: Lesson 9 complete â€” Making appointments
key_points: Suggest: 'Would [day/time] suit you?' / 'Are you free on...?'
Accept: 'That works perfectly.' / 'I'll put it in my calendar.'
Decline politely: 'I'm afraid I'm busy then â€” could we try...?'
Confirm: 'Just to confirm â€” [day, time, place]'
Remind: 'Just a gentle reminder about our meeting tomorrow.'
After WOULD: base verb â€” 'Would it suit you?' not 'suits'
